cards.back: Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) is a statistical technique used to compare means of three or more groups to determine if at least one group mean is different from the others.
The basic principle of ANOVA is to test the null hypothesis that all group means are equal against the alternative hypothesis that at least one group mean is different from the others.

One-way ANOVA is a type of ANOVA used to compare means of two or more groups to determine if at least one group mean is different from the others, when there are only two levels of the independent variable.

Two-way ANOVA is a type of ANOVA used to compare means of three or more groups to determine if at least one group mean is different from the others, when there are two levels of the independent variable and possibly one or more levels of a second independent variable.

A Latin-square design is a type of experimental design used in ANOVA, where each treatment is applied to each level of the independent variable in a square arrangement, ensuring that each treatment is applied an equal number of times.
